public.title: The Halloway Vault
public.tagline: A vault that only opens for the right numbers opened at two in the morning for somebody who had them.
public.author: rd_case_desk
public.question: Who emptied the Halloway vault?

lines.text: Premises: Marlowe County Savings, Court Square branch. Vault opened and emptied of bearer bonds series B-112 through B-140 on the night of Sunday the 9th. Night porter found locked in the coal cellar, unharmed.
The public is invited to believe the vault was 'cracked.' Nothing about this door was cracked. It was asked politely, in its own language.
File note: the auditor's October worksheets were signed out Friday and have not been returned.
Statement of G. Halloway (public record, excerpt): 'I was at the Elks' fish fry in Dorsett and heard of it by telephone at four.'
Canvass note: three separate depositors volunteered that 'the old cracksman over the barbershop' must have done it. None could say how he would dial a telephone, let alone a vault.
Timetable appendix: the 4:05 milk train is the only northbound movement between midnight and six.
Sundry: the barbershop chair, the fever ward, and the river reeds keep better hours than the bank.

suspects.name: Gordon Halloway
suspects.blurb: Manager of Marlowe County Savings — his name is on the door and the shortfall is on his desk. The Row likes a boss for a bank job; it saves thinking.

suspects.name: Mabel Quist
suspects.blurb: Head teller, fourteen years at the cage. Knows every drawer, every habit, every officer's signature — and exactly what she has never been allowed to know.

suspects.name: Arthur "Doc" Renz
suspects.blurb: Retired safecracker living over the barbershop. Did seven years for the Merchants' Trust job. Swears the game left him before his hands did.

suspects.name: Silas Nash
suspects.blurb: The night auditor, a careful man with a green eyeshade who counts other people's money and initials every page. Writes with his left hand and speaks to no one.

shards.text: The time-lock disc stopped when the door swung: 2:11 a.m., Sunday night into Monday.
The porter, from the coal cellar: footsteps overhead 'a while after two — unhurried, like a man who owned the floor.'
Beat officer tried the Court Square doors at 1:50 — all fast, all dark, nothing stirring.
Lock examiner: dialed clean and opened on the first pass. No drill, no soup, no fishing marks in the wards.
Photographs: dial face unmarked, jamb unsprung, hinges undisturbed. The door was operated, not attacked.
Tumbler wear is consistent with one practiced entry — no trial runs, no hesitation drag on the third wheel.
Insurance examiner concurs: 'a key job in everything but the key — whoever dialed it held the true numbers.'
Branch memo, Friday: working combination changed at close of business per quarterly schedule.
Vault log: Friday's combination change witnessed and countersigned by two officers.
Head office: a relief combination was cut during the July audit and held under seal against lock-out — the only other numbers that door has ever answered to.
Seal register: the relief-combination envelope logged intact month by month since July — the October line is blank.
The porter's one glimpse through the coal grate: lamp held high in the right hand, the dial worked with the left.
Smudge drag across the ledger shelf runs right to left — a left hand steadying paper.
Polish smears on the dial rim arc counterclockwise, the sweep of a left-handed grip.
A cigarette stubbed cold on the floor — left of the stool, where a left-handed man rests his working arm.
Stationery inventory, Thursday: one fiber sample-case missing from the supply closet — noted, not chased.
The porter: 'past two I heard case latches snap twice, sharp, like a drummer packing up.'
Milk-train conductor: a man boarded the 4:05 north with a fiber sample-case and rode standing, holding it on his knees in the vestibule.
Monday noon: the fiber case ashore in the river reeds below the trestle — empty, latches sprung.
Elks club steward, Dorsett: the fish fry ran long; Halloway was at the head table until the hall emptied at three.
Raffle record: Halloway's ticket called at 2:45 — he answered from the floor and took the smoked ham to general applause.
Road ferry log: Halloway's sedan crossed back toward the city at 3:20 — the forty-minute side of the river.
Dorsett constable: walked Halloway to his car at three and watched him take the ferry road.
Policy letter, head office: branch managers barred from holding relief combinations since the reforms of '44.
Audit committee minute, August: Halloway's request to hold the relief numbers 'against emergency' DENIED, noted with some asperity.
The '44 shortage inquiry, final page: the reform that took combinations out of managers' hands — and gave the sealing of them to the audit function.
Branch counsel: only two copies of the relief numbers existed — head office's, and the audit-room working copy made in July.
Exchange supervisor's log: M. Quist worked the relief switchboard Sunday night, plugged in from ten till four.
Timed subscriber tickets at 2:15 and 2:50, both in Quist's hand, both initialed at the board.
The operator on the next board: 'Mabel never left her chair except the once for coffee, and I held her cords the four minutes.'
Tram conductor: took Miss Quist home on the 4:10 owl car, same as every relief Sunday.
Procedure card, teller cage: tellers bank blind — floats counted in by an officer each dawn; no teller holds vault numbers.
Quist's July requisition for vault access to speed the morning count: DENIED, per policy, initialed by the auditor.
Head cashier: 'In fourteen years Mabel has never once been inside the vault door. That is the system working, not a slight.'
The barber: 'I hold Doc's cup for him. Have done for two winters. He can't close his fingers on the handle.'
Pension examiner's report: knuckles fused with arthritis; no fine work possible; cannot manage buttons unassisted.
Telephone company note: all of Renz's calls are operator-placed — he cannot work a dial of any kind, and says so bitterly.
County General admissions: Renz, A., taken in Saturday noon with influenza fever, ward six.
Ward sister: 'He never left ward six Sunday night. I turned his pillow at two and he cursed me for it at three.'
Visitor book: his nephew signed in Sunday evening and was sent away — 'patient sleeping, no visitors past eight.'

contradictions.note: The working combination died Friday at close — and Sunday night the door opened clean on the first pass. Whatever was dialed, it was not the numbers that no longer existed.
The case rode north full at 4:05 and came ashore south, empty, below the trestle. It did not swim. Someone brought it back the way auditors travel.

truth.motive: Nash cut the relief combination himself during the July audit and initialed the seal he later broke. Fourteen years of counting other men's money, one October page left blank, and a left-handed man let himself in politely.
truth.summary: Silas Nash, the night auditor, opened the Halloway vault. The relief combination cut in July lived in the audit-room copy; the October seal line is blank; the man at the dial worked left-handed by lamplight and rode the 4:05 with the bank's own sample case.
reveal: The vault opened at 2:11 for the true numbers — two days after the working combination died.
Only the July relief combination was live. Managers were barred from it in '44. Tellers bank blind. The old cracksman was in ward six with hands that cannot dial.
The man at the dial worked left-handed and carried the bonds out in the bank's own fiber case, north on the 4:05.
The audit-room copy of the relief numbers was made in July, sealed in July — and the October seal line is blank.
Silas Nash counted the bank's money by night, cut its spare key in July, and wrote the whole story left-handed. The blank line in the seal register is his signature.

facts.text: The vault was opened between two and three, Sunday night.
It was opened with a true combination — no force, no fishing, first pass.
The working combination died Friday; only the July relief combination was live that night.
The man at the dial worked left-handed.
The bonds left in the bank's own fiber sample-case, north on the 4:05.
Halloway was at the Dorsett fish fry until three, the forty-minute side of the ferry.
Halloway never held the relief combination — policy since '44, his own request denied.
Quist was plugged into the exchange switchboard until four, voices and tickets to prove it.
Quist has never held or seen a vault combination — tellers bank blind.
Renz's hands are ruined — he cannot work a telephone dial, let alone a vault.
Renz was in the County General fever ward from Saturday noon.
